main_summary.summary: Annie Pearl Avery, born in Birmingham, Alabama, traces her path into the civil rights movement from a Pittsburgh childhood spent with her great-aunt, where she encountered integrated schooling and absorbed news of Emmett Till's 1955 murder. Returning to Birmingham, she followed the 1959 lynching of Mack Charles Parker in Poplarville, Mississippi, briefly engaged with the Nation of Islam through Muhammad Speaks coverage, and in 1961 attempted to join the Freedom Rides at age 16. Wilson Brown, who told her she did not understand nonviolence, brought her to a SNCC meeting in Atlanta where she met Ella Baker, James Forman, Ruby Doris Smith, Julian Bond, Bob Zellner, Dottie Miller Zellner, Penny Patch, and Bill Hansen. She participated in the first sit-in in Birmingham with Reverend Fred Shuttlesworth, was arrested in Albany, Georgia, marched alone in Gadsden, Alabama to complete William Moore's interrupted walk, drew a 90-day sentence for going limp in a Danville, Virginia courtroom, served as project director in Hale County, Alabama, and worked voter registration in Natchez and Clarksdale, Mississippi. She was the only person arrested on Bloody Sunday at the Edmund Pettus Bridge on March 7, 1965, after a physical disagreement with a state trooper.
key_themes: Voter registration in Alabama and Mississippi
Nonviolence as tactic versus way of life
Bloody Sunday at the Edmund Pettus Bridge
main_summary.historical_significance: Avery's testimony documents the rank-and-file SNCC field experience that linked the 1961 Freedom Rides to the 1965 Selma campaign through continuous direct action in Birmingham, Albany, Danville, Gadsden, Hale County, Clarksdale, and Natchez. Her account of Wyatt Tee Walker assigning her to discreetly warn Dr. King about state troopers seeking to serve an injunction at the A.G. Gaston Motel, and her recollection of Hosea Williams and Andrew Young flipping a coin to determine SCLC's representative on the Bloody Sunday march after Katzenbach's office warned that King's life was in danger, preserve operational details of movement decision-making rarely captured in published narratives.

chapters.title: Interview Opening in Selma
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, with filmmaker John Bishop, opens the Civil Rights History Project interview in Selma, Alabama on May 31, 2011, welcoming Annie Pearl Avery to discuss her life and engagement with SNCC.

chapters.title: Born in Birmingham, Sent to Pittsburgh
chapters.summary: Avery describes her birth in Birmingham, Alabama, her father being 54 at her birth, and being the first of four children. Her childless great-aunt in Hazelwood, Pittsburgh, recently widowed, wanted company, so her mother sent her north to live there for a few years.

chapters.title: A Nickel for the Emmett Till Story
chapters.summary: Living on Sylvan Avenue and attending Gladstone Elementary in Pittsburgh, Avery was nine or ten when Emmett Till was murdered in 1955. With no newspaper at home, she paid a nickel at a neighborhood grocery store to read about the killing, understanding only that a Negro child had been murdered.

chapters.title: Mack Charles Parker and Birmingham Ministers
chapters.summary: Back in Birmingham at fourteen, Avery followed the 1959 lynching of Mack Charles Parker, taken from jail and killed in Poplarville, Mississippi. She recalls the Montgomery bus boycott, Autherine Lucy at the University of Alabama, and Birmingham ministers Reverend Shuttlesworth, Reverend Charles Billups, Reverend Oliver, and Reverend J.L. Phifer working to desegregate Phillips High School.

chapters.title: Buying a Ticket and a Knife
chapters.summary: At sixteen, excited by news of young people in the movement, Avery purchased a Trailways ticket from Birmingham to Montgomery to join the Freedom Rides and bought a knife to protect herself on the bus. She recalls the bus being attacked in Anniston, Alabama, and her own preference for the familiar Greyhound station her grandfather frequented.

chapters.title: Wilson Brown Explains Nonviolence
chapters.summary: At the roped-off Greyhound station, Avery met Wilson Brown, part of a Birmingham welcoming committee for the Freedom Riders. When she said she wanted to go, Brown told her she could not because she did not understand nonviolence, explaining that when someone hits you, you cannot hit back. She got a refund for her ticket.

chapters.title: The Nation of Islam and Muhammad Speaks
chapters.summary: Avery describes her first contact with the Nation of Islam when Mack Charles Parker was jailed, drawn by Muhammad Speaks as the only paper covering his case daily. She attended some meetings but found the organization not visibly active enough, and turned toward the sit-ins and Freedom Rides for more direct action.

chapters.title: Life on the Back of the Bus
chapters.summary: Asked about her Birmingham life before the Atlanta meeting, Avery contrasts riding the back of the bus with her Pittsburgh years, where her aunt told her she could sit anywhere and Northern Black children corrected her speech, telling her to say pop instead of drink. Returning to segregation in Birmingham helped motivate her to get involved.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: First SNCC Meeting in Atlanta
chapters.summary: Wilson Brown invited Avery to a Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee meeting in Atlanta, where she left her knife at home. There she met her first decent white people, including Anne Braden, Bob Zellner, Dottie Miller Zellner, Penny Patch, and Bill Hansen, and Black organizers Ms. Ella Baker, James Forman, Ruby Doris Smith, and Julian Bond.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: Confused and Stopped in Marietta
chapters.summary: Heading back from Atlanta, Avery, Wilson Brown, Nathaniel Lee, and a white girl who wanted to meet Reverend Shuttlesworth got confused and ended up in Marietta, Georgia. Seen as a mixed group, they were followed by police, who stopped and arrested Wilson, impounded the car, and left the others on the street with no way to get around.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: Becoming Customers at the Bus Station
chapters.summary: Unable to find any Black community, the group returned to the Marietta bus station, where an attendant said they could not stay because they were not customers. Recalling interstate commerce law, Avery had them buy tickets to become customers so they could not be removed, using the payphones and switchboard to reach the SNCC office.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: A Hostile White Crowd Gathers
chapters.summary: As darkness fell, a growing white crowd packed the Marietta station. The three planned to take refuge in the phone booths if things worsened. Word reached the press, and the Associated Press began calling the payphone every few minutes to check whether they were still safe.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: Rescue from Marietta at Midnight
chapters.summary: Around midnight the crowd grew hostile, beating on seats with sticks and shouting threats to kill them. James Forman, Julian Bond, attorney Howard Moore, and people Avery believed were from the Justice Department arrived from Atlanta in dark government cars, got Wilson out of jail, and decided the group should stay in Atlanta overnight rather than risk Birmingham.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: Meeting Shuttlesworth and the First Sit-In
chapters.summary: Back in Birmingham, Avery met Reverend Shuttlesworth at his home in his pajamas. He took her to what she calls Birmingham's first sit-in at a Woolworth's lunch counter, where a white man went down the counter spitting and pouring ketchup. Not yet adjusted to nonviolence, she was made to stand beside Shuttlesworth and Reverend Charles Billups.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: Arrested in Albany, Georgia
chapters.summary: Avery traveled to Albany, Georgia, where she was arrested and met attorney C.B. King along with Rutha Mae Harris, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Charles Sherrod, Bob Mants, and Reverend Prathia Hall. She describes herself as in a learning stage, saying little and absorbing from the experienced and often more educated organizers around her.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: Emotions of Protest and Being Free
chapters.summary: Reflecting on the mix of emotions during hostile protests, Avery says she did not walk around angry, having been exposed to a different life in the North. She invokes the idea that once you have been free you do not want to be a slave again, which she believes shaped her subconscious drive to act.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: Volunteer Pay and Ten Dollars a Week
chapters.summary: Avery explains that SNCC volunteers were unpaid, staying in people's homes, helping with housework, doing voter registration, and joining demonstrations. She suspects the government saw the lack of pay as a way to discredit SNCC, and recalls workers eventually being given about ten dollars a week.

chapters.part: Into SNCC and the First Arrests
chapters.title: Adapting to Jail in Albany
chapters.summary: Avery recounts going out for voter registration one day in Albany and being locked up the next after a demonstration, jailed three or four weeks. She describes putting the experience in the back of her mind and adapting to her environment, obtaining cigarettes and reading material, a skill she says she has been good at in and out of jail.

chapters.title: Finishing William Moore's March Alone
chapters.summary: After postal worker William Moore was killed completing his solo march, Avery decided to pick up a sign and finish what he started, even alone, not realizing she was recreating his solitary walk. She was arrested minutes after she began walking and was placed in jail in Gadsden, Alabama.

chapters.title: Diane Nash and Madeleine Sherwood
chapters.summary: The day or two after her Gadsden arrest, other women were jailed, including Diane Nash and actress Madeleine Sherwood, who had played the pregnant woman in Cat on a Hot Tin Roof and was bonded out right away as the white woman in the group. Avery refused to give interrogators any information about how she got there or who brought her.

chapters.title: The Hole at Tutwiler Prison
chapters.summary: Citing a threat to take the women from jail, authorities sent the men to Kilby and moved the women to Tutwiler prison, where they first spent a night crawling in a dark sealed hole. The next morning they were placed in the hospital ward, where a trustee from Tuscaloosa serving a life sentence recognized them as the freedom fighters everybody knew about.

chapters.title: Nonviolence as Tactic, Not a Way of Life
chapters.summary: Avery distinguishes herself from those who took nonviolence on as a way of life, saying she was among those who accepted it only as a tactic. She frames this as a matter of individual conviction within the movement rather than a single shared philosophy.

chapters.title: The Decoy March in Birmingham
chapters.summary: Avery recounts a Birmingham demonstration where Wyatt Tee Walker had her join a decoy march of ten or fifteen people to throw the police off before a larger march came later. She tells of a friend who first refused to go but joined the second march rather than be left out once others went.

chapters.title: Waking the Sleeping Judge in Danville
chapters.summary: Working in Danville, Virginia, Avery and Freedom Singer Matthew Jones, alongside Avon Rollins and an SCLC worker, arrived at a crowded courthouse to find the judge asleep during the case. They went limp at the front of the podium to wake him; when an officer explained it was called going limp, the angry judge gave them 90 days for contempt.

chapters.title: Awakened by the 16th Street Bombing
chapters.summary: Released by project director Avon Rollins to take a break, Avery returned to Birmingham late on a Saturday. The next morning she was awakened by the bombing of the 16th Street Baptist Church, about sixteen blocks from her home, and walked to the scene near the A.G. Gaston Motel.

chapters.title: Gathering at the Gaston Motel
chapters.summary: At the roped-off A.G. Gaston Motel, Avery saw Ms. Ella Baker, Dr. King, Reverend Abernathy, Jim Forman, and Andrew Young, and met a Jet editor for the first time. Emotional over the murdered children, the group sat in a room that night discussing more aggressive direct action that was not violent but went beyond marching and going to jail.

chapters.title: Project Director in Hale County
chapters.summary: Avery describes settling into Hale County as project director for voter registration, finding a house to rent and letting the community know who they were and why they came. She recalls meeting an Army sergeant who said he did not believe in nonviolence, yet stood outside the first mass meeting to make sure everyone was safe.

chapters.title: The Ten-Gauge Shotgun as Self-Defense
chapters.summary: The community gave the workers a ten-gauge shotgun, and St. Clair, who also taught Avery to drive, taught her to use it behind their rented house. She frames the gun as a psychological deterrent against cowardly Klansmen, calling it self-defense rather than violence, and says they never had to use it.

chapters.title: From Clarksdale to Natchez
chapters.summary: Avery recalls another tight situation where Chico Neblett was project director and the workers slept in a woman's rented room because no one would house them, until St. Clair bought a cheap house. She then came to organize voter registration in Natchez, Mississippi, where she was arrested, after working in Clarksdale.

chapters.title: The Coin Flip Before Bloody Sunday
chapters.summary: After Jimmie Lee Jackson was killed, the plan to carry his body to Selma became a march. The night before, a call from Attorney General Katzenbach's office warned that Martin's life was in danger, so King was taken away, and Hosea Williams and Andrew Young flipped a coin to decide who would represent SCLC, which is how Williams led alongside John Lewis, Amelia Boynton, and Marie Foster.

chapters.title: The Only Arrest on the Bridge
chapters.summary: Avery explains that the troopers' intent on Bloody Sunday was to terrorize and brutalize the marchers into not marching, lining up past the first traffic light beyond the Edmund Pettus Bridge. After scouting the route, she was the only person arrested, jailed following a physical disagreement with a policeman over where she would stand, while most marchers faced beatings and tear gas.

chapters.title: Warning Dr. King at the Gaston Motel
chapters.summary: Avery recounts being chosen by Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker for a discreet task while the State Patrol searched the A.G. Gaston Motel to serve Dr. King an injunction. Walker whispered for her to go to the room where King was staying with Andy and Bernard Lee, rather than the registered master suite, and quietly tell them police were looking for him.

chapters.title: Bonded Out by Peter Hall
chapters.summary: Avery describes Bloody Sunday as the most vivid memory of the three bridge marches and notes it was the shortest time she ever spent in jail. Birmingham attorney Peter Hall, one of their lawyers, drove in and bonded her out of his own pocket, returning to Birmingham before authorities finally released her near midnight.

chapters.title: An African Name in the White House
chapters.summary: Avery shares her reaction to the election of the first African American president, saying she never expected it in her lifetime and assumed any such president would have a name like Charles or John rather than an African name. The election fell on her sixtieth birthday, which she took as a turning point and a sign things could not get better.

chapters.title: Sixteen and Joining the Movement
chapters.summary: Clarifying the timeline, Avery confirms she was sixteen at her first Atlanta meeting, turning seventeen that November, so she was seventeen when first arrested. The interviewer and Avery settle that her first SNCC meeting was in 1961, making her a very young person quickly moving into significant roles.

chapters.title: A Modern-Day Harriet Tubman in Jail
chapters.summary: Avery reflects that she was later called a modern-day Harriet Tubman, though she did not know who Tubman was at the time. She describes the psychological shock of the iron jail door and how she kept others from breaking down, since a crying jag invited jailer retaliation and made other prisoners think they could be run over.

chapters.title: Appearing Strong Behind Bars
chapters.summary: Avery explains the small economies of jail survival: pooling fifty cents for cigarettes, securing cards or dominoes, and slipping out phone numbers so someone could check on a situation. She emphasizes that it was better to appear strong than weak, a discipline she practiced before realizing it echoed Harriet Tubman.

chapters.title: SNCC and CORE as the Infantry
chapters.summary: Comparing the organizations, Avery says SCLC, SNCC, CORE, and the NAACP each had a place, but older people moved slowly while the young wanted change now. She frames SNCC and CORE as the infantry who entered places where you could be arrested just for walking the street or for vagrancy.

chapters.title: Forman as Mentor, Friends in the Field
chapters.summary: Avery names Jim Forman as a mentor and lists the people she worked with personally, including Janet Moses, Ruby Sales, Gwen Patton, Bob Mants, Ruby Doris Smith, Prathia Hall, Sam Block, James Peacock, Willie Peacock, Frank Smith, Charles Sherrod, and Charles Jones, recalling Sam Block and the Peacocks from Mississippi.

chapters.title: Judy Richardson and Bonds Forged in Jail
chapters.summary: Avery describes Judy Richardson, who came from California and worked mostly out of the Atlanta office, as one of her best friends, recalling going to jail with her. She notes the deep bonds formed by those who spent time in jail together, observing you really get to know someone over ten weeks behind bars.

chapters.title: The Klan Surrounds the Clarksdale Office
chapters.summary: Avery recounts the most frightening night of her movement life in Clarksdale, Mississippi, when the Klan surrounded the SNCC office during a curfew on Black people. Unable to leave or see faces, the workers could only see headlights and hear screaming and slurs all night, taking cover under desks. Lafayette Surney headed the project there.

chapters.title: Staying with SNCC After 1965
chapters.summary: Asked about her role after 1965, Avery says many dropped off, expecting things would get better. She went to New York around 1967 and worked from the New York SNCC office, remaining with SNCC partly because SCLC, made up of older and more mature people, did not put her on staff.

chapters.title: Black Power as Lifting Ourselves Up
chapters.summary: Avery returned to Atlanta to run a tax service and frames the late-1960s shift toward self-defense and Black Power, which she calls radical and culturally conscious. She argues Black Power was about lifting Black people up, not the violence many assumed, distinguishing the self-defense of Hale County from overt violence.

chapters.title: Meeting Mandela in Selma
chapters.summary: Avery describes learning African American and African history as part of thinking well of herself, recalling her arrest in Atlanta for trying to see an African dignitary. She finally met one without being arrested when Nelson Mandela came to Selma and Rose Sanders introduced them, and she hugged him with gratitude.

chapters.title: Honored to Have Been Among Them
chapters.summary: Closing the interview, Avery says it was an honor to be associated with the SCLC, NAACP, and CORE people, and with those now working with the Bridge Crossing Jubilee, the National Voting Rights Museum and Institute, and the Mississippi Civil Rights Veterans. The interviewers thank her, and the recording ends with the Library of Congress and Smithsonian credit.

loc_video.caption: Annie Pearl Avery oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Selma, Alabama, 2011 May 31
